title: What is Single-Label Classification?
concept: Single-label classification assigns exactly one category from a set of mutually exclusive choices to each image.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In previous phases, when we classified an image as either a 'dog' or 'cat', how many labels could a single image have?

options.text: Multiple labels at the same time

exercises.explain: Correct! Standard classification models assign exactly one mutually exclusive category to each input image.

cards.title: Choosing one flavor
cards.body: Imagine ordering a single scoop of ice cream. You must choose exactly one flavor — chocolate, vanilla, or strawberry. You cannot mix them. This is the essence of single-label classification.

cards.title: Mutually exclusive choices
cards.body: Single-label classification is a task where a model assigns exactly one label from a predefined set of categories to an input image. The categories are assumed to be mutually exclusive.

cards.title: Sorting mail into bins
cards.body: Think of sorting letters into post office boxes. Each letter goes into exactly one box based on its destination. A letter cannot be in two boxes at once.

cards.title: MNIST Digit Recognition
cards.body: In the famous MNIST dataset, the goal is to classify handwritten digits (0 through 9). A single written character is always exactly one digit — it cannot be both a 3 and an 8.

cards.title: No grey areas allowed
cards.body: You might think a model can output '50% dog, 50% cat' to mean the image contains both. In single-label classification, this output actually means the model is uncertain which single category is correct.

left.text:
Classes: [Cat, Dog, Bird]
One winning label per image
Softmax forces sum to 1

right.text:
Classes: [Cat, Dog, Bird]
Can have 0, 1, or all labels
Independent Sigmoid outputs

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks is a classic example of single-label classification?
exercises.explain: A house number digit is exactly one character, making it a single-label task.

options.text: Identifying the single primary digit in a house number photo
Tagging all ingredients visible in a pizza photo
Detecting all cars, pedestrians, and signs on a street
Generating a text caption describing an outdoor scene

exercises.prompt: True or false: In single-label classification, the target categories must be mutually exclusive.
exercises.explain: True. The core assumption is that each image belongs to exactly one category, meaning they are mutually exclusive.
Mutually exclusive means that if one category is true, the others must be false.
exercises.sentence: Single-label models assume that categories are mutually ___.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction output represents a single-label classification model?
exercises.explain: Predicting one class with a confidence score is the standard output of a single-label model.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to sort fruits on a conveyor belt into apples, oranges, or bananas. Each fruit is photographed individually. Should you use single-label classification?

options.text: Yes, because each photo contains exactly one fruit which belongs to one category.
No, because a fruit could be both an apple and an orange.
Yes, but only if the model predicts the coordinates of the fruit first.
No, because we need to predict the weight of the fruit.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since each photo contains only one fruit and the categories are mutually exclusive, single-label classification is the perfect tool. Next, let's look at the mathematical function that makes this possible!
